core.intro: Lilly pilly is really two plants in one: a fast, dense evergreen hedge and a producer of small edible berries. The same basics apply wherever you garden: a spot in sun to part shade, most reasonably drained soils, and a named variety chosen for its growth habit and its resistance to the pimple psyllid. The state sections cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety: hedge, bush food, or both
sections.body: Lilly pilly is a common name shared across a whole group of Australian rainforest trees in the myrtle family, most of them now placed in the genus Syzygium (the old genus Acmena has been folded in). The trade sells Syzygium australe (brush cherry, the parent of most hedging cultivars), Syzygium smithii (the former Acmena smithii, the original common lilly pilly), Syzygium luehmannii (riberry, the premium bush food), Syzygium paniculatum (magenta lilly pilly), Syzygium oleosum (blue lilly pilly), Syzygium moorei (coolamon) and the broad-leaved Acmena hemilampra. Every lilly pilly berry is edible and none are poisonous, but flavour varies a lot, so decide first whether you want a tidy screen, the best fruit, or both, then pick a named form to match. Lilly pillies sit in the same family as jaboticaba and guava.

sections.heading: Psyllid resistance: the one thing to check
sections.body: The make-or-break factor when buying a lilly pilly is resistance to the pimple psyllid, a tiny sap-sucking insect whose nymphs raise pimple-like bumps and distort the soft new growth of susceptible plants. Old seedling Syzygium australe and the magenta lilly pilly are the worst affected, while the former Acmena types (Syzygium smithii) and riberry are naturally resistant and rarely marked. That is exactly why nurseries sell so many named, psyllid-resistant selections of Syzygium australe. The simple rule: choose a Syzygium smithii or a riberry for natural resistance, or buy a named resistant selection rather than an unnamed seedling. The in-stock table above shows which named forms are available right now.

sections.heading: Pollination: one plant is enough
sections.body: Lilly pillies are pollinated by bees and other insects drawn to their fluffy, nectar-rich flowers, and a single plant will set fruit on its own, so you do not need to buy two. The magenta lilly pilly goes a step further and can set seed without fertilisation at all, which is one reason solitary garden specimens still fruit reliably. If your only interest is a clipped hedge this hardly matters, but it is worth knowing that one plant left to flower will give you a crop.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Lilly pillies take full sun to fairly heavy shade and grow in most soils that are not waterlogged, from sands to clay loams. They are tougher than their rainforest origin suggests: an established plant tolerates moderate frost and extended dry spells, though young plants and the riberry are more frost-tender and want shelter in cold districts for their first couple of winters. Improve poor soils with compost and a good mulch, and keep new plants watered until the roots get down. Container-grown plants can go in for most of the year as long as you water through summer (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Water young plants through their first summers to establish a deep root system, after which most lilly pillies are fairly drought hardy, though a hedge under stress thins out and is more prone to psyllid, so do not let it go bone dry. Feeding is where lilly pillies surprise people: they are in the myrtle family, not the protea family, so unlike phosphorus-sensitive natives such as grevilleas and banksias they are not harmed by ordinary phosphorus and take a normal complete or controlled-release fertiliser. They prefer rich soil and assured moisture, so feed in spring, and again after a summer trim on a hedge, to drive the dense new growth that makes a good screen. A surface mulch of compost or well-rotted manure does the same job more slowly.

sections.heading: Pruning and hedging
sections.body: This is the lilly pilly's great strength: it responds extremely well to pruning, reshoots readily from old wood, and can be clipped into a formal hedge, a screen, a ball or a standard. That is why it is the default fast evergreen hedge across much of Australia. A light tip-prune of the soft new growth both shapes the plant and, on resistant types, keeps it dense; on a screen, trim after each main flush. Hard renovation pruning is tolerated too, so an overgrown plant can be cut back and will come away again.

sections.heading: Harvest and eating
sections.body: Lilly pillies flower mostly in spring and summer and ripen their berries from summer into autumn and winter, only a few months from flower, with the exact window depending on the species and your climate. The fruit is eaten fresh but is mostly tart or crisp rather than sweet, so it shines once processed: all of it is high in pectin and makes excellent jam, jelly, sauce and syrup. Riberry is the standout, with a tart, cranberry-like flavour and a hint of cloves, and is the basis of a small commercial bush food industry; blue lilly pilly is also well regarded fresh. Expect to share the crop with birds, which strip the berries and are part of the plant's appeal in a habitat garden.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named plant grown from a cutting rather than an unnamed seedling. Named cultivars are propagated from cuttings, which strike readily and come true to type, so you get the exact growth habit, leaf colour and psyllid resistance on the label, while seedlings are a lottery on all three. Check two things before you buy: the species or cultivar's psyllid resistance, and its mature size, since compact selections hold at one to two metres for a low hedge or pot while screening and forest types run to five metres or much more. The in-stock table above lists the named forms available now and what they cost.

faqs.q: Are lilly pilly berries safe to eat?
faqs.a: Yes. No lilly pilly berry is poisonous, so the fruit is safe to pick and taste straight off the bush. The difference between varieties is flavour, not safety: some are pleasant raw while others are sharp or bland and are better cooked. Riberry and blue lilly pilly are the ones most worth seeking out for eating fresh.

faqs.q: How big does a lilly pilly get, and can I keep it small?
faqs.a: It depends entirely on the form you buy. Compact modern selections sit around one to two metres and suit a low hedge or a pot, screening types hold at three to five metres, and a few forest species reach ten metres or more if never cut. Because they all take trimming so well, you can hold almost any lilly pilly to the height you want.

faqs.q: Can I grow a lilly pilly in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes. The compact cultivars are well suited to a large pot on a courtyard or balcony, where they make a tidy evergreen feature or a clipped ball. Keep the water up because pots dry out fast, feed through the warmer months since nutrients wash out of containers, and pot up or refresh the top of the mix every couple of years.

faqs.q: How fast does a lilly pilly grow, and when will it fruit?
faqs.a: They are quick growers, often putting on half a metre or more a year once established, which is what makes them such a fast screen. A plant grown from a cutting of a named form can flower and fruit within a few years, but a hedge that is clipped often produces little fruit because the flower buds are trimmed off, so leave at least part of the plant unclipped if you want a crop.

NSW.intro: Growing lilly pilly in New South Wales. NSW is the heartland of the group: every common species grows wild here, the rare magenta lilly pilly is found nowhere else on earth, and the riberry bush food trade began on the north coast. It is also the default hedge plant of coastal gardens from the Tweed to the far south coast.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The warm-temperate to subtropical NSW coast suits every lilly pilly, which is why they are planted so heavily from Sydney northwards. Brush cherry ranges naturally from the Queensland border down to the south coast, so it is at home across the settled parts of the state. The cooler, frostier tablelands are harder on young plants and on the tender riberry, and the humid north coast carries the most psyllid and disease pressure, so match the species to your district.

sections.heading: Where lilly pilly grows in NSW
sections.body: The Northern Rivers around Lismore, Byron Bay, Kyogle and Murwillumbah is the bush food heartland, where riberry was first grown in plantation and where Daleys is based. The Mid North Coast around Coffs Harbour and the Macleay River marks the southern edge of the wild riberry range and carries small native-food farms. Further south, the magenta lilly pilly survives in coastal pockets, and Sydney gardens make lilly pilly the most planted screening hedge in the state.

sections.heading: The magenta lilly pilly, a NSW endemic
sections.body: The magenta lilly pilly (Syzygium paniculatum) grows wild only in New South Wales, in a thin band of coastal rainforest from near Taree in the north to Jervis Bay in the south, with its largest stands around the Central Coast. It is listed as vulnerable nationally and endangered in NSW, so the wild trees are protected, but nursery-grown plants are widely sold. It has good, sweet, magenta berries and was the original edible lilly pilly, though it is one of the more psyllid-prone species, so look for a selected, resistant clone.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: On the warm coast riberry ripens through summer, roughly December to February, while brush cherry and the common lilly pilly carry their berries into autumn. In the cooler tablelands and the south, ripening runs later. Because the plants flower over a long spring and summer, a single bush can carry fruit at several stages at once.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: The pimple psyllid is at its worst on the humid north coast, so resistant varieties earn their keep there. Myrtle rust, an exotic fungus of the myrtle family, was first found in Australia on the NSW Central Coast in 2010 and is now established along the coast, attacking the soft new growth of susceptible lilly pillies. Queensland fruit fly is endemic in the warmer parts of the state and is a recorded host of lilly pilly and riberry, so it can blemish the berries.

faqs.q: Which lilly pilly makes the best hedge for a Sydney garden?
faqs.a: For a low-fuss screen, lean on the naturally psyllid-resistant former Acmena types (Syzygium smithii), such as the narrow upright selections sold for hedging, or a named resistant brush cherry. They give a dense, fast, evergreen wall and shrug off the psyllid that disfigures older unnamed plants. Match the cultivar's mature height to the hedge you want so you are not fighting its natural size.

faqs.q: Is it legal to grow the magenta lilly pilly at home?
faqs.a: Yes. It is the wild populations of magenta lilly pilly that are protected, not nursery-grown plants, which are propagated and sold freely. Growing one in your garden is fine and even helps conserve the species. Choose a selected clone for better psyllid resistance, and enjoy what is probably the sweetest of the lilly pilly berries.

QLD.intro: Growing lilly pilly in Queensland. Queensland is part of the plant's native home, and its warm, humid climate grows the fastest, densest hedges in the country. The same warmth and humidity bring the heaviest psyllid and myrtle rust pressure, so a resistant variety is worth the extra effort to find.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Queensland's warm, frost-free coast is ideal for fast lilly pilly growth, and several species are native here: brush cherry ranges north to Mossman, and riberry grows in coastal rainforest up to the Cairns hinterland. A hedge that takes years to thicken in the cool south can fill in within a couple of seasons on the Queensland coast. The trade-off is humidity, which favours the pimple psyllid and fungal disease, so health and variety choice matter more here than almost anywhere.

sections.heading: Where lilly pilly grows in QLD
sections.body: Lilly pilly is grown as a hedge right along the populated coast, with major plant nurseries on the Sunshine Coast north of Brisbane. The wettest, most productive rainforest end of the range sits on the Atherton Tableland behind Cairns, while the Scenic Rim and border ranges south of Brisbane, including Mount Glorious, hold the southern Queensland stands of riberry and coolamon. Warm coastal gardens everywhere use lilly pilly as a quick screen.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Queensland opens the lilly pilly season ahead of the southern states. Riberry ripens through summer, around December to February, and the warm climate brings the brush cherry and other species into fruit early too. In the wet tropics the long warm season can give more than one flush of flower and fruit in a year.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Humidity is the theme. The pimple psyllid thrives in the warm coastal air, so resistant former Acmena types and named selections are the safe choice. Myrtle rust is established in Queensland and attacks the new growth, flowers and fruit of susceptible myrtles, including lilly pillies, so keep plants healthy and treat early outbreaks. Queensland fruit fly is endemic and is a recorded host of lilly pilly and riberry, so it can sting the ripening berries.

faqs.q: Which lilly pilly copes best with Queensland humidity?
faqs.a: Choose for psyllid resistance, because the humid coast is where the pimple psyllid is worst. The naturally resistant former Acmena lilly pillies (Syzygium smithii) and the riberry shrug it off, and there are named, bred-for-resistance brush cherries if you want that look. Avoid cheap, unnamed seedling stock, which is the most likely to end up pimpled and disfigured.

faqs.q: Is riberry worth growing for bush food in Queensland?
faqs.a: Yes. Riberry is native to coastal Queensland, grows quickly in the warm climate, and is the most useful lilly pilly for the kitchen, with a tart, clove-like flavour that makes excellent jam, sauce and syrup. One plant fruits on its own, so a single tree in a warm, sheltered spot will keep a household in riberries, with plenty left over for the birds.

VIC.intro: Growing lilly pilly in Victoria. Only one species, the common lilly pilly (the former Acmena smithii), reaches Victoria in the wild, but the whole group is planted across Melbourne and the state as a hardy evergreen hedge that takes the cold far better than its tropical cousins.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria's cooler climate slows lilly pilly growth, which is no bad thing for a hedge, and the common lilly pilly is hardy here, taking moderate frost once it is established. Riberry and young plants of any species are more frost-tender, so cold inland gardens lean on the tough common lilly pilly and keep tropical types in the warmest spots. The cooler, drier air also eases the psyllid and disease pressure that troubles the humid north.

sections.heading: Where lilly pilly grows in VIC
sections.body: In the wild, lilly pilly reaches its southern limit in Victoria. The common lilly pilly grows naturally in the sheltered river gullies of East Gippsland, east of the Snowy River, with a famous isolated stand far to the south on Wilsons Promontory, the southernmost natural lilly pilly in Australia. As a garden plant it is grown right across Melbourne and the Mornington Peninsula, where it is one of the most popular screening hedges.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victoria ripens its lilly pilly fruit latest of the mainland states, because the cool season slows everything down. The common lilly pilly flowers through summer and carries its berries into autumn and winter. Most Victorian lilly pillies are grown as hedges rather than for fruit, so a lot of the crop is trimmed off before it can ripen.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Psyllid pressure is lighter in Victoria's cooler, drier climate than on the warm humid coast, though susceptible brush cherries can still be marked, so resistant types are still the smart pick. Myrtle rust is present in Victoria, so inspect the new growth and remove any leaves with the tell-tale bright yellow pustules. Birds are the main competitor for the berries, stripping a ripe bush quickly.

sections.heading: Frost on young plants
sections.body: Even the hardy common lilly pilly is tender while young, and the riberry stays frost-sensitive for life. In frost-prone Victorian districts, protect new plants through their first winters with a sheltered, north-facing position and frost cloth on the coldest nights, and start with the cold-hardy common lilly pilly rather than a tropical species.

faqs.q: Which lilly pilly is hardiest for a cold Melbourne garden?
faqs.a: The common lilly pilly, the species once called Acmena smithii, is the cold-hardiest and the one native as far south as Victoria, so it is the safe choice for a frosty garden. Avoid the riberry and the more tropical selections in cold districts, since they stay frost-tender. Give any new plant a sheltered spot for its first couple of winters.

faqs.q: Why is my lilly pilly's new growth spotted or dying back in Victoria?
faqs.a: Two culprits are likely. Bright yellow, powdery pustules on the soft new growth are myrtle rust, a fungal disease of the myrtle family that is present in Victoria; prune out and bag affected growth and avoid wetting the foliage. Pimple-like bumps and twisted leaves instead point to the pimple psyllid. Either way, healthy plants and resistant varieties cope best.

WA.intro: Growing lilly pilly in Western Australia. Lilly pilly is not a WA native, but it is one of the most popular evergreen hedge plants in Perth and the south-west, and the state's dry climate gives it one real advantage: WA has so far kept out the myrtle rust disease that troubles the eastern states.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Lilly pilly suits the warm, dry Mediterranean climate of south-west WA provided it gets summer water, since it comes from the wetter east coast and will not thrive bone dry. It is a hardy plant, grown in gardens across most of the settled south-west, and frost is only a minor issue near the coast, though young plants and the riberry want protection from cold in inland districts. The dry summers keep the pimple psyllid and fungal disease far quieter here than in the humid east.

sections.heading: A myrtle in a rust-free corner
sections.body: Myrtle rust, the exotic fungus that attacks lilly pillies and other myrtles right along the eastern seaboard, has only been reported from a remote part of northern WA and has not reached the populated south-west. It is a declared pest in WA and must be reported if found, and the state's biosecurity rules are built around keeping it out. For a WA gardener this means your lilly pillies are, for now, free of the single worst disease their eastern cousins face.

sections.heading: Where lilly pilly grows in WA
sections.body: As an introduced ornamental from eastern Australia, lilly pilly is planted as a hedge across greater Perth and the Swan Coastal Plain, through the cooler, wetter South West around Margaret River and Bunbury, and in the Mandurah and Peel district south of the city. The popular forms are psyllid-resistant brush cherry selections grown for screening. There is no wild WA population, so every plant here is a garden one.

sections.heading: Pests in WA
sections.body: With myrtle rust kept out and the climate dry, WA lilly pillies are an easy plant. The pimple psyllid can still appear, raising bumps on the new growth of susceptible brush cherries, so a resistant former Acmena type or a named selection avoids most trouble. Mediterranean fruit fly is endemic across WA, but lilly pilly is not on its list of host fruits, so the berries are rarely stung even though medfly spoils so many other WA crops. Scale and sooty mould turn up on stressed or shaded plants, and the cure is to fix the vigour and airflow rather than to spray.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: Here is the catch for WA buyers: because lilly pilly is a myrtle, and WA is working hard to keep myrtle rust out, live myrtle-family plants sent from the eastern states must meet strict import conditions and many interstate nurseries simply will not post them across. The practical answer is to buy from a WA grower, which also gives you stock already suited to the local climate. The table above is filtered to sellers that can supply plants here.

faqs.q: Will a lilly pilly hedge cope with Perth's sandy soil and dry summers?
faqs.a: Yes, with help while it settles in. Dig plenty of compost into the sand, mulch thickly, and water deeply through the first couple of summers so the roots get down, after which lilly pilly is reasonably drought hardy. Feed in spring to push the dense growth that makes a good screen, and use a soil wetting agent if your sand has turned water-repellent.

faqs.q: Will a lilly pilly drop staining berries onto my paving?
faqs.a: It can, so it pays to plan for it. Ripe lilly pilly berries are soft and can stain paving, decking and pool surrounds when they fall, so keep a fruiting plant back from paths or grow it as a clipped hedge, since regular trimming takes off most of the flower buds and most of the fruit with them. If you want the berries for the kitchen, plant where a bit of drop does not matter.
